div.h1: Benchmark Results: SIMD vs Scalar vs libc

g.text: memset Benchmark Results (Intel i7, DDR4-3200, CPU pinned 2.4GHz)
Note: Streaming used for >64KB where cache bypass provides measurable gains.

div.p: You won't beat libc's memset/memcpy on production systems. The purpose of hand-written SIMD is to master the patterns libc uses:

ul.li: Instruction density optimization Master them here to build algorithms libc doesn't provide: string scanning, custom filters, and matrix math.

g.text: 1. libc wins everywhere: glibc memset uses AVX-512, REP STOSB, and CPU dispatch
2. SSE2 beats scalar 4-5× for buffers >1KB where loop overhead dominates
3. Memory bandwidth bound at >1MB: all converge to ~25 GB/s (DDR4-3200)
4. Streaming stores win at >16MB: non-temporal stores bypass cache

text.tspan: // Plus REP STOSB for tiny buffers
// Plus REP STOSB for tiny buffers

div.p: Overhead-Dominated libc wins due to specialized small-buffer paths (REP STOSB, etc.)
SSE2 Advantage Zone Hand-written SIMD beats naive scalar, but libc still wins with AVX/AVX-512
Streaming Wins Non-temporal stores bypass cache, winning for buffers > L3
